prompt: Your team must classify thousands of support tickets per hour on a tight budget; quality needs are moderate and latency matters. Which model strategy fits best?
choices: Fine-tune a large model on your historical tickets before processing any new ones
Send every ticket to your most capable frontier model so accuracy is never a concern
Use a small, fast model for the bulk and escalate only the low-confidence cases to a larger model
Classify the first batch, then reuse those answers for all later tickets to avoid new calls
explanation: Right call. A small fast model for the routine bulk, escalating only uncertain cases to a bigger one, holds cost and latency down while protecting quality where it matters.

prompt: Your AI feature's API bill is climbing sharply even though request counts are flat. What should you investigate first?
choices: Tokens per request — longer prompts and larger outputs raise cost even when traffic is constant
The number of concurrent users, since cost scales with users rather than tokens
Whether the provider quietly moved your requests from GPU to CPU hardware
The size of your vector-database index, which is what providers bill for
explanation: Good instinct. LLM usage is billed per token, so heavier prompts or more verbose outputs inflate the bill even at flat traffic — trim context and cap output length first.

prompt: You're building an assistant that must reason over a 200-page contract that exceeds the model's context window. What's the right design?
choices: Send the whole document and rely on the model to ignore the irrelevant parts
Chunk the document and retrieve only the passages relevant to each question (RAG)
Summarize the entire contract once and answer every future question only from that summary
Switch to a model with a smaller context window to force the answer to stay focused
explanation: Exactly the pattern. When content exceeds the window, chunk it and retrieve only the relevant passages per question — you stay within limits and the answer stays focused.

prompt: You want an LLM to generate unit tests for an existing function. Which prompt most reliably produces correct, useful tests?
choices: Describe the function from memory in plain English and ask for tests
Ask for the test names first, then have it fill in the bodies without seeing the code
Have it also rewrite the function from scratch so the tests match its own version
Provide the actual function code, the expected behavior, and the test framework you use
explanation: Yes. Grounding the model in the real code, the expected behavior, and your framework yields tests that compile and assert the right things; ungrounded prompts drift.

prompt: A PM wants the AI to turn vague feature ideas into user stories. How should you prompt for consistent, usable output?
choices: Send only the feature title and let the model infer the audience and outcome
Give the persona, the goal, and an explicit acceptance-criteria template, and ask for stories in that structure
Ask for free-form prose so each story reads uniquely and naturally
Tell it to omit acceptance criteria to keep the stories short
explanation: Well structured. Persona, goal, and an explicit acceptance-criteria format give the model the scaffold for consistent, ready-to-use stories instead of a different shape each time.

prompt: Your customer-facing chatbot occasionally emits toxic or off-topic replies. What's the right design response?
choices: Lower the temperature so the model is less likely to wander off-topic
Add a disclaimer warning users that some answers may be inappropriate
Add input and output guardrails that validate, filter, and constrain responses before users see them
Retry each request several times and return the shortest reply
explanation: Solid. Guardrails that validate and constrain both inputs and outputs are the systematic fix — lowering temperature or adding a disclaimer doesn't actually stop unsafe content from reaching users.

prompt: An employee wants to paste a spreadsheet of customer names, emails, and account numbers into a public AI tool to summarize it. What should policy direct them to do?
choices: Redact or avoid the PII, and use an approved tool covered by a data-handling agreement
Allow it as long as they delete the chat history afterward
Allow it but add a line to the prompt asking the tool not to store the data
Allow it if the spreadsheet is under a few hundred rows
explanation: Good judgment. The risk is the PII leaving your control; redact it and route the task to an approved tool with real data terms — deleting the chat or asking nicely in the prompt doesn't govern retention.

prompt: Teams across the company are quietly adopting unapproved AI tools ('shadow AI'). What's the most effective governance response?
choices: Block all external AI tools at the network and consider the problem solved
Require a manager's email sign-off before anyone may use any AI tool
Wait for an incident, then write a restrictive policy in response
Provide sanctioned, well-supported tools plus clear usage guidelines so people don't need to go around IT
explanation: Mature thinking. Shadow AI grows when there's no good sanctioned option; offering approved tools with clear guidance channels demand safely, where blanket blocks just push it out of sight.

prompt: You want an assistant that can look up live order status, check inventory, and issue refunds. What capability does this require?
choices: A larger context window so all order data fits in the prompt
Fine-tuning the model on last quarter's order history
A retrieval (RAG) index over past orders so it can read them
Tool use / function calling so the model can invoke your real APIs through a controlled interface
explanation: Sharp distinction. Acting on live systems — not just reading about them — requires tool use / function calling. RAG retrieves text; it can't actually issue a refund.

prompt: You're giving an autonomous agent the ability to run shell commands and edit files. What's the most important guardrail before going live?
choices: Give it broad access so it never stalls on a permission error
Scope its permissions tightly and require human confirmation for irreversible or destructive actions
Let it run unattended but email you a summary of what it did afterward
Cap the model's output length so its commands stay short
explanation: Safety-first, exactly. Least-privilege scoping plus a human gate on destructive, hard-to-reverse actions keeps an agent from causing real damage — an after-the-fact summary is too late.

prompt: Your coding assistant keeps inventing internal API methods that don't exist in your codebase. What design change fixes the root cause?
choices: Ground it in your actual repository via retrieval (RAG) so it answers from real code
Add a system instruction telling it to never invent methods
Raise the temperature so it explores more method options
Switch to a model with a more recent training cutoff
explanation: Right at the root. The model never saw your private code, so the durable fix is retrieval that puts real repository code in front of it — a 'don't invent' rule or a newer model can't supply knowledge it never had.

prompt: Before shipping an AI feature that summarizes legal documents, how should you judge whether it's good enough?
choices: Rely on the model provider's published benchmark scores
Have one senior engineer read a couple of outputs and approve
Build a task-specific eval set of representative inputs with known-good outputs and measure against it
Ship to a small group and act only if complaints come in
explanation: Rigorous. A task-specific eval set with representative inputs and expected outputs is the reliable pre-release signal — vendor benchmarks and a quick eyeball don't tell you how it does on your data.

prompt: An interactive AI feature feels sluggish, hurting UX. Which change best improves perceived latency without changing the answer?
choices: Stream the response token-by-token so users see output immediately, and cache common results
Route every request to your largest model so it finishes thinking sooner
Batch several users' requests together before each call
Raise the max output tokens so the model never stops early
explanation: Good UX instinct. Streaming output as it's generated, plus caching repeats, makes the feature feel fast without touching answer quality; batching helps throughput but delays an interactive reply.

prompt: Leadership asks whether to build a custom in-house model or use a hosted AI API for a new feature. What should drive the decision?
choices: Always build in-house to avoid depending on any external vendor
Always buy, since training a model in-house is never worthwhile
Match whatever the largest competitor in your industry chose
Whether the capability is core to your differentiation and whether you have the data, talent, and budget to sustain it — otherwise buy
explanation: Sound strategy. Build-vs-buy turns on whether the capability is truly core and sustainable for you — most teams should buy/use an API unless it's strategic differentiation they can resource long-term.

prompt: You're scaling an AI feature from pilot to all users and must justify continued investment. What should you put in place to track ROI?
choices: Track total monthly spend and keep it under a fixed cap
Assume ROI is positive because the pilot demo impressed everyone
Tie cost per request to a concrete value metric (tickets deflected, hours saved) and watch it as usage grows
Report the model's benchmark accuracy as the ROI figure
explanation: Good discipline. Pairing cost per request with a concrete value metric and tracking it as you scale is how you prove ROI and catch unit economics that quietly break at volume.

prompt: A long AI coding session is nearing the model's context limit but the task isn't done. How do you continue without losing what matters?
choices: Keep going and let the oldest messages fall out of the window automatically
Summarize the key decisions and current state into a compact handoff, then continue from that
Paste the entire raw transcript into a fresh session and continue
Lower the temperature so the model retains more of the earlier history
explanation: Good context hygiene. A compact summary/handoff of the decisions and current state preserves what matters in far fewer tokens; re-pasting the raw transcript just hits the same limit again.

prompt: An AI PR-review bot handles ~800 PRs/month. Each call averages ~6,000 input tokens and ~1,000 output tokens, and output is priced higher than input. What most directly determines the monthly bill?
choices: Mainly the input tokens you send, since the bot's own comments aren't charged
Mainly the PR count; the size of each diff doesn't materially affect cost
Total tokens (input + output) across all calls, with output costed at its higher rate
The model's parameter count, which sets the price
explanation: That's the calculation. Cost is total tokens across all calls — input and output priced separately, output weighted higher — which lets you forecast and control the bill. Output is not free.

prompt: A downstream program must reliably parse your model's output. What's the most robust way to design this?
choices: Let the model reply in natural language and parse it with regular expressions
Ask for output in a strict, specified JSON schema and validate it before use, repairing or rejecting on failure
Add 'please stay consistent' to the prompt and trust the format to hold
Set temperature to zero so the wording never varies
explanation: Robust by design. A defined JSON schema plus validation gives downstream code a contract to rely on; regexing free text or hoping for consistency breaks the first time the phrasing shifts.

prompt: You want an assistant to reach several internal systems (a wiki, a ticket tracker, a database) through one consistent, reusable interface instead of bespoke one-off integrations. What fits best?
choices: Paste each system's full contents into the context window on every call
Fine-tune the model nightly on each system's data
Write a separate hard-coded integration inside the prompt for each system
Expose each system through a standard tool/context protocol (e.g. MCP) so the assistant connects uniformly and reusably
explanation: Clean and scalable. A standard protocol like MCP exposes tools and data sources through one consistent interface the assistant reuses — far better than brittle one-off integrations or stuffing everything into the prompt.

prompt: You tweak a production prompt to fix one bad case. How do you avoid silently breaking other cases you can't directly see?
choices: Version the prompt and run it against a saved set of test cases (an eval/regression set) before rolling it out
Deploy it right away, since the case you just tested now works
Keep the prompt inline in the code and keep adjusting it in production as issues surface
Rely on remembering which earlier phrasings worked
explanation: Treat prompts like code. Version them and run a saved regression/eval set before rollout, so a fix for one case can't quietly break ten others you didn't re-check.
